rationale: The two papers are tightly aligned on customizable product option decisions: the cited study tests additive vs. subtractive option framing effects on option uptake, spending, and decision difficulty, while the citing study models how customization design features affect product utility and perceived complexity. The citing note also says it integrates choice-task complexity and loss-aversion ideas, which are central to the cited paper’s framing account, so this is plausibly a theory/mechanism source rather than an incidental mention.
